items.explanation:
11th President (1845–1849), Democrat
"Dark horse" candidate — first major surprise nominee in U.S. presidential history
Promised to serve only one term and kept it — one of the few presidents to voluntarily limit himself
Acquired Oregon Territory through negotiation with Britain and California/Southwest via the Mexican-American War
Oversaw the greatest territorial expansion since the Louisiana Purchase — added more land than any president except Jefferson
Died just 103 days after leaving office, the shortest retirement of any president
Considered the last strong president before the Civil War era
Signed the act establishing the Smithsonian Institution (1846)

12th President (1849–1850), Whig
Nicknamed "Old Rough and Ready" for his informal style and battlefield toughness
Hero of the Mexican-American War, especially the Battle of Buena Vista (1847)
Never voted in an election before being elected president himself
Died just 16 months into his term — possibly from gastroenteritis after eating cherries and iced milk at a July 4th celebration
Owned slaves but opposed the expansion of slavery into new territories
Second president to die in office (after William Henry Harrison)
His body was exhumed in 1991 to test for arsenic poisoning — results were negative

13th President (1850–1853), Whig
Last Whig president — the party collapsed shortly after his term
Assumed the presidency after Zachary Taylor's sudden death
Signed the Compromise of 1850, including the controversial Fugitive Slave Act
Often credited with installing the first bathtub and kitchen stove in the White House, though these claims are disputed
Later ran for president on the anti-immigrant Know-Nothing Party ticket in 1856
Helped found the University of Buffalo
Refused an honorary degree from Oxford, reportedly saying he had neither the literary nor scientific attainment to deserve it

14th President (1853–1857), Democrat
Only president from New Hampshire
Consistently ranked among the worst presidents by historians
Signed the Kansas-Nebraska Act (1854), which led to the violent conflict known as "Bleeding Kansas"
His 11-year-old son Bennie was killed in a train accident weeks before the inauguration — Pierce and his wife witnessed the death
The only elected president not nominated for a second term by his own party
Arrested during his presidency for running over a woman with his horse (charges were dropped)
Close college friend of Nathaniel Hawthorne, who wrote his campaign biography

15th President (1857–1861), Democrat
Only president from Pennsylvania and the only president who never married (lifelong bachelor)
Served in Polk's Cabinet as Secretary of State and later as U.S. Ambassador to the United Kingdom
Presidency saw the Dred Scott Supreme Court decision (1857) and the secession crisis that led to the Civil War
Widely regarded as one of the worst presidents for failing to prevent the breakup of the Union
His niece Harriet Lane served as First Lady in the absence of a presidential spouse
Last president born in the 18th century (born 1791)

16th President (1861–1865), Republican
Preserved the Union through the Civil War and issued the Emancipation Proclamation (1863)
Delivered the Gettysburg Address, one of the most famous speeches in American history
First Republican president ever elected
Assassinated by John Wilkes Booth at Ford's Theatre on April 14, 1865 — died the next morning
Tallest president at 6'4" and largely self-educated as a frontier lawyer from Illinois
Established Thanksgiving as a permanent national holiday (1863)
Face on the $5 bill and the penny, and carved into Mount Rushmore

17th President (1865–1869), National Union/Democrat
First president to be impeached — acquitted by a single vote in the Senate
Assumed office after Lincoln's assassination; clashed bitterly with Radical Republicans over Reconstruction
Vetoed the Civil Rights Act of 1866, but Congress overrode the veto — the first major legislation passed over a presidential veto
Purchased Alaska from Russia for $7.2 million, a deal mocked as "Seward's Folly"
Never attended a single day of school — his wife taught him to read and write
Only former president to later serve in the U.S. Senate (elected 1875), until Andrew Johnson held that distinction alone among presidents

18th President (1869–1877), Republican
Commanded all Union forces in the Civil War and accepted Lee's surrender at Appomattox Court House (1865)
Real name was Hiram Ulysses Grant — "U.S." came from a clerical error at West Point that stuck
Presidency plagued by scandals including Credit Mobilier and the Whiskey Ring, though Grant himself was not personally corrupt
Established Yellowstone as the first national park (1872)
Wrote his memoirs while dying of throat cancer — published by Mark Twain, they became a critical and financial success
Face on the $50 bill
Served two full terms despite the scandals

19th President (1877–1881), Republican
Won the most disputed election in U.S. history (1876) — decided by a congressional commission after contested electoral votes
Nicknamed "Rutherfraud" by opponents who considered his presidency illegitimate
Ended Reconstruction by withdrawing federal troops from the South as part of the compromise that gave him the presidency
First president to have a telephone in the White House, installed by Alexander Graham Bell himself
Banned alcohol from the White House — his wife Frances was nicknamed "Lemonade Lucy"
Pledged to serve only one term and kept his promise

20th President (1881), Republican
Could simultaneously write in Latin with one hand and Greek with the other — he was ambidextrous
Assassinated by Charles Guiteau after only 200 days in office — shot on July 2, 1881 and died September 19, 1881
His death led directly to the Pendleton Civil Service Reform Act (1883), ending the spoils system
Was a Civil War general and the only sitting member of the House of Representatives ever elected president
Last president born in a log cabin
Last of four presidents to die in office up to that point (Harrison, Taylor, Lincoln, Garfield)
